notable.notable_blurb: Yale-educated Revolutionary lieutenant, Farmington town clerk

cross_connections.display_label: His wife's great-great-great-grandfather had defended Tunxis land rights in 1738.
Both Tunxis-related officials in early 1800s Farmington.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College graduate prior to enlistment in 1775. Yale education preceded his career as Farmington town clerk + judge of probate + state representative.

military_service.unit: Wyllys's 3rd CT (1777) → Butler's 2nd CT (1778) → Webb's regiment in the Highlands (1781-1783)
military_service.notes: January 1777: entered the Continental Army as Ensign in Colonel Samuel Wyllys's Third Connecticut Regiment. April 1778: promoted to Lieutenant and Adjutant of Colonel Zebulon Butler's Second Connecticut Regiment, and afterwards acted as Quartermaster. From January 1781 to the close of the war was with Colonel Samuel B. Webb's regiment in the Highlands. Discharged September 1783. Member, Connecticut Society of the Cincinnati (served as Secretary until the Society's 1804 dissolution).

education.notes: Graduate of Yale College prior to Revolutionary War service.

career.title: Secretary of the Connecticut Society of the Cincinnati
career.notes: Served as Secretary of the Connecticut Society of the Cincinnati until the Society's dissolution in 1804.

career.title: Town Clerk
career.notes: Town Clerk of Farmington CT for 32 years from 1791 to 1823.

career.title: Representative to the Connecticut General Assembly
career.notes: Represented Farmington in the Connecticut General Assembly across 42 sessions from 1795 to 1820. Presented an expense account to the legislature in 1795. In 1802 was authorized by the legislature to sell land belonging to Molly Meazon, a Tunxis Indian.

career.title: Judge of Probate
career.notes: Judge of the Farmington Probate District from 1810 to 1820.

career.title: Justice of the Peace and Quorum
career.notes: Sat on the bench of the Hartford County Court as Justice of the Peace and Quorum for many years.

burial.plot_notes: Find a Grave — Memento Mori Cemetery

narrative_blocks.header: Authorized to sell a Tunxis woman's land
narrative_blocks.body: In 1802 the Connecticut General Assembly authorized John Mix to sell land belonging to Molly Meazon, a Tunxis Indian — one of several entanglements between Mix's official roles and Native land transactions. He had been involved earlier in a suspicious 1789 land deal over Daniel Mossuck's property. The Mix family's civic prominence in Farmington was woven through these dispossessions.

narrative_blocks.header: Spent his last years in total blindness
narrative_blocks.body: After decades on the bench and in the General Assembly, John Mix lived his final years in total blindness in Farmington. He died April 29, 1834 at age seventy-nine. His estate was appraised at $6,671.

narrative_blocks.header: Forty-two sessions in the Connecticut legislature
narrative_blocks.body: John Mix represented Farmington in the Connecticut General Assembly across forty-two separate sessions from 1795 to 1820 — a quarter-century legislative tenure. Concurrently he served thirty-two years as Farmington town clerk and ten years as Judge of Probate, three roles overlapping for most of his post-Revolutionary life.

narrative_blocks.header: Silver knee buckles and a quick temper
narrative_blocks.body: Farmington's town historian Mr. Gay described him as 'tall in stature, dressed as a gentleman of the time, with silver knee buckles, formal in manner, of quick temper, punctilious, very hospitable, a good neighbor, a member of no church, and bound by no creed, and in politics a federalist.'

narrative_blocks.header: Justice signed manumission fourteen years after Tunxis
narrative_blocks.body: On January 10, 1816 Justice of the Peace John Mix examined Titus, a Black man held by Capt. Joshua Youngs of Farmington, and certified him 'desirous of being made free.' Mix signed the certificate of examination, witnessed Joshua Youngs's deed of emancipation, and recorded the deed as Register the same day. Same hand, same Hartford County office, fourteen years after the Tunxis sale he had certified.

bio.notes: Middle name Hooker honors maternal grandmother Martha Hooker Cowles
bio.title: Captain

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' carries his maternal grandmother Martha Hooker Cowles's maiden surname forward.

career.title: Supercargo, East India trade
career.notes: In his younger days he was a supercargo of ships in the East India trade — managing cargo + commercial transactions on long voyages.

career.title: Ship captain, East India trade
career.notes: Subsequently commanded ships in the East India trade.

burial.plot_notes: Find a Grave — Memento Mori Cemetery

narrative_blocks.header: East India trade captain became Farmington's quaint sailor
narrative_blocks.body: In his younger days Ebenezer was supercargo on East India trade ships and later commanded those ships himself. As his years increased and he retired from business, he became one of the notable characters of Farmington — a town better known for ministers and politicians than for sea captains.

sources.short: Find a Grave — Memento Mori Cemetery Farmington CT

naming_inspiration.description: Named for paternal grandmother Anna Goodwin Mix (mother of John Mix). After her 1783 death at age 3, her parents named the next daughter (HD0524, born 1784) Ann Goodwin a second time.

sources.short: Find a Grave — Memento Mori Cemetery Farmington CT
Find a Grave — Memento Mori Cemetery Farmington CT

cross_connections.display_label: Her husband was her half-blood second cousin once removed via Capt. Isaac Cowles.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her older sister Ann Goodwin Mix #1 (HD0975), who died at age 3 the year before this Ann Goodwin's 1784 birth — classic replacement-naming pattern.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost two infants then died at forty
narrative_blocks.body: Ann married Dr. Harry Wadsworth in 1807 and bore three children — Harry Jr. in 1806, Theodore in 1807 (lost as an infant the next year), and Betsey Mix in 1809 (lost at age two). Her husband died in 1813, leaving her widowed with one surviving child. She died at forty in 1824.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her second cousin through Cowles line
narrative_blocks.body: Ann's 1807 marriage to Dr. Harry Wadsworth crossed a hundred-year Cowles loop — both descended from Capt. Isaac Cowles of Farmington (1675-1756). Ann came down through Isaac's third-wife son Ezekiel; Harry came down through Isaac's first-wife son Isaac II via Mary Cowles Wadsworth. Half-blood second cousins once removed.

parents.mother_research_notes: Elizabeth 'Betsey' Allen Wadsworth (1757-1806, not built per Jalapeño — Allen line outside Hooker tree). EH 1909 referred to her as 'Betsey' (period nickname for Elizabeth) without maiden surname; full identification per WikiTree.

cross_connections.display_label: His wife was his half-blood second cousin once removed via Capt. Isaac Cowles.

career.title: Physician
career.notes: Practicing physician at Farmington CT per EH 1909.

narrative_blocks.header: A Farmington doctor dead at thirty-two
narrative_blocks.body: Harry Wadsworth practiced medicine at Farmington and married Ann Goodwin Mix in 1807. He died at thirty-two in 1813, having already buried two of their three children, and left Ann a widow with only their son Harry surviving.

career.title: Physician
career.notes: Physician (M.D.) per EH 1909. Migrated to Austinburg NY where he died at age 36-37.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his father Dr. Harry Wadsworth — Jr. carried the same first name + same medical profession.

narrative_blocks.header: Father's namesake physician died young in New York
narrative_blocks.body: Harry Jr. carried his physician father's first name and medical profession, but died at age thirty-six in Austinburg New York in 1843 — only thirty years after his own father's premature death in 1813. He was the only one of Ann Goodwin Mix's three children to reach adulthood.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for paternal grandfather Dr. Theodore Wadsworth of Southington CT.
Named 'Betsey Mix' — middle name 'Mix' carries her mother's maiden surname; first name 'Betsey' likely echoing maternal aunt Betsey Mix (HD0976) who was still living in 1809.

sources.notes: The Williams Family — Rev. Joshua Williams + descendants.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale Class of 1805 — second-generation Yale graduate following father Rev. Joshua Williams (Yale 1780).

career.title: Pastor of the Congregational Church
career.organization: Congregational Church of the Upper Houses (later Cromwell CT)
career.notes: Ordained and instituted pastor at the Upper Houses (now Cromwell Middlesex County CT) on June 10, 1809 — his father Rev. Joshua Williams Sr. assisted in his ordination. Spoken of locally as 'priest Williams.' Owned the house built by Rev. Gershom Bulkeley, later known as the 'Joseph Edwards House.' Pastor for 23 years until his death.

career.title: Founding President of the Friendly Association
career.notes: Organized the Friendly Association, was its first president, and active in its support throughout his life.

career.title: Scribe of the General Association of Connecticut
career.notes: Served as scribe at the 1825 Litchfield meeting of the General Association of Connecticut.

education.notes: Graduated Yale 1805 — followed his father's 1780 Yale graduation.

narrative_blocks.header: Father and Yale-student son buried in one grave
narrative_blocks.body: Joshua Lewis died December 29, 1832 in the Upper Houses at age 47. The next day his eldest son John Mix Williams — a seventeen-year-old Yale student — also died. Father and son were buried in one grave January 1, 1833 per Joshua Lewis's own written instruction, though they were given separate headstones.

education.notes: Yale student at time of death December 30, 1832 at age 16-17.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale student at time of death (December 30, 1832, day after father's death). Did not complete degree.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his maternal grandfather Lt. John Mix (X00263, 1755-1834), Yale-educated Revolutionary War lieutenant and 32-year Farmington town clerk.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale student died the day after his father
narrative_blocks.body: John was a seventeen-year-old Yale student when his father Rev. Joshua Lewis Williams died December 29, 1832 in the Upper Houses. John died the very next day, December 30. Per his father's written wish, both were buried in one grave January 1, 1833, with separate headstones.

sources.notes: The Williams Family — Rev. Joshua Williams + descendants.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his father Rev. Joshua Lewis Williams (X00266); also continuing his paternal grandfather's name (Rev. Joshua Williams Sr., X02770).

sources.notes: The Williams Family — Rev. Joshua Williams + descendants.
The Williams Family — Rev. Joshua Williams + descendants.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' carries the Hooker ancestry forward — same naming pattern her grandmother Martha Cowles Mix's children used (Ebenezer Hooker Mix HD0523, Martha Hooker Mix HD0980).

narrative_blocks.header: Seven children carried Hooker, Mix, Wadsworth ancestral names
narrative_blocks.body: Catherine had seven children whose names trace the family's intermarriages: Edward Hooker Deming and her own middle name carry Hooker ancestry; John Mix Deming carries her maternal grandfather's name; Harry Wadsworth Deming carries the Wadsworth surname through her aunt Ann Goodwin Mix's marriage. Three children died young; four reached adulthood.

sources.notes: The Williams Family — Rev. Joshua Williams + descendants.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Hooker' carries her maternal grandmother Martha Hooker Cowles's maiden surname forward — same naming pattern as her oldest brother Ebenezer Hooker Mix.

sources.short: Find a Grave — Memento Mori Cemetery Farmington CT

burialCemetery.notes: Historic burying ground in Farmington Connecticut, founded 1661 — one of the oldest cemeteries in the state. Resting place of John Mix (X00263, 1755-1834, Revolutionary War lieutenant and 32-year Farmington town clerk) and his wife Martha Cowles Mix (H00437, 1753-1826), with most of their eight children — Capt. Ebenezer Hooker Mix (the East India sailor character of Farmington), Betsey, Catherine Williams, John William, and Martha Hooker — alongside Ann Goodwin (the first, lost at age 3) and the boy William (lost at age 4). Ann Goodwin No. 2 also rests here.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST115.name: Society of the Cincinnati
INST115.notes: Hereditary organization founded 1783 by officers of the Continental Army at the close of the Revolutionary War — named for Cincinnatus, the Roman general who returned to his farm after victory. Hooker-tree members documented in Yale archives and in EH 1909: Maj. Roger Hooker (H00306, Continental Line major), Capt. Simeon Newell (X00258, Roger's brother-in-law via Mercy Hooker Newell H00307), John Mix (X00263, Revolutionary officer of Farmington CT — son of Ebenezer and Anna Goodwin Mix), Aaron Burr Jr. (H00913, original member), and Adm. George Dewey (TD0141, honorary member elected 1901 by the New York Society chapter). | Merged from INST071: Hereditary organization founded May 13, 1783 by officers of the Continental Army and their French allies. Named for Lucius Quinctius Cincinnatus, the Roman general-farmer who returned to his plow after saving Rome. Original members from the Hooker tree: Maj. Roger Hooker (H00306) of Farmington CT; Capt. Simeon Newell (X00258) of Southington CT, whose brother-in-law was Hooker.

Both Tunxis-related officials in early 1800s Farmington.
